title: Verifiable Format Control for Large Language Model Generations
authors: Zhaoyang Wang, Jinqi Jiang* (equal contribution), Huichi Zhou* (equal contribution), Wenhao Zheng, Xuchao Zhang, Chetan Bansal, Huaxiu Yao. Affiliations: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill; Imperial College London; Microsoft Research.
venue: arXiv preprint (cs.CL), v1 submitted 6 February 2025; arXiv comments field states "To appear at Findings of NAACL 2025" (i.e., Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: NAACL 2025). Paper internally names its dataset/method "VFF" (Verifiable Format Following).
claim: 7B-level open-source LLMs (e.g., Mistral-7B, LLaMA-2-7B, LLaMA-3-8B) satisfy general instruction-following reasonably well but fail badly at fine-grained format control, especially as the number of composed constraints increases (VFF level-1 to level-3). The paper curates VFF, a fully Python-verifiable format-following dataset built from ~60 meta constraints instantiated over Alpaca questions, and shows that a self-improvement pipeline (sample responses -> annotate correctness with the verifiable Python checkers -> SFT then DPO, applied progressively from level-1 to level-3 difficulty) substantially raises 7B models' format-following accuracy on both in-domain VFF and out-of-domain IFEval, sometimes surpassing GPT-4 on level-3 VFF, with modest side effects on general instruction following (InfoBench).
novelty: Authors frame the contribution as: (1) a dataset (VFF) that is fully verifiable by Python function per sample rather than by human or LLM judges, distinguishing it from prior instruction-following benchmarks (Table 1: Alpaca/Vicuna/PandaLM/WizardLM = human-judged; LLM-EVAL/INFOBENCH/Conifer/FOFO = LLM-judged, single-level; only IFEval and VFF are automatically (Python) verifiable, and IFEval, FollowBench, FCS, and VFF all carry the multi-level (ML) mark — per Table 1's own checkmarks, IFEval and VFF have an identical mark pattern (Verifiable=yes, ML=yes, HJ=no, LJ=no, AJ=yes), so VFF is NOT the sole benchmark combining Python-verifiability with multi-level composed constraints; IFEval already does. VFF's actual distinguishing claims relative to IFEval, per the paper's own prose, are broader constraint-category coverage, e.g. JSON-format output (Sec 2.1: 'our dataset covers more types of format constraints such as JSON output') and dataset scale (|Q| x sum|V_k| combinatorial size, Sec 3.1)); (2) a progressive self-improvement training pipeline that uses the verifiable checker as a free, GPT-4-free reward/annotation signal for SFT+DPO, scaling difficulty from level-1 (single constraint) to level-3 (up to 3 composed constraints) so each stage trains on the prior stage's checkpoint; (3) an empirical demonstration (Section 5.3) that Python-based judgment strictly dominates LLM-based judgment (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o) on accuracy, cost, and speed, and that LLM judges are meaningfully inconsistent across repeated queries even at low temperature.

protocol.description: VFF is a single-turn generation benchmark: the model receives one instruction (question + concatenated constraint text) and produces one free-text response in one forward pass, which is then checked by Python functions. There is no multi-turn/interactive evaluation loop for VFF itself; the only 'multi-step' element in the paper is on the training side (progressive training across difficulty levels, and one-shot demonstration used only during the training-data self-improvement sampling stage), not in how a trained/evaluated model is queried at test time.
protocol.observation_content: unverified — missing from extraction. The exact rendered instruction string (question + concatenated meta-constraint text) is only shown in Figures 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 (e.g., Fig.1 'An example with level-3 constraints of VFF', Fig.9 'Example of level-3 instruction with three different format constraints') and in Algorithm 1's pseudocode box, all of which are image/diagram content not extractable as text in this local render (see 'Figures not reproduced' note in the source file). The paper's prose describes the construction abstractly: 'each instruction sample x of VFF dataset consists of a detailed question coupled with several constraint instances... x = [q, d] where d includes c unique constraint instances' (Sec 3.1, Format Following Dataset), and Appendix A.2 describes example instances in prose (e.g., a level-2 instruction requiring 'three verbs synonymous with "to apologize"' plus 'exactly three sentences'; a level-3 instruction requiring 'sentence starting with a specific letter,' 'exactly 6 paragraphs,' and 'between 50 and 110 words'), but the literal prompt template/format string the model sees is not present in the extracted text.

representations: unverified — missing from extraction. No ablation over input/prompt representation formats (e.g., JSON-schema vs. natural-language constraint listing, ordering of constraints, presence/absence of few-shot demonstrations at evaluation time) is reported in the extracted text. The only sampling-format-adjacent comparison found is Table 4 (Sec 5.2), which compares 'Direct' (sample k times without demonstration) vs. one-shot-demonstration sampling for training-data collection, not for evaluation-time prompting; see headline_numbers.

models.name: GPT-3.5 / GPT-4-turbo
models.strategies: unverified — missing from extraction (no stated CoT/few-shot/algorithm-in-prompt strategy annotations; used as reference baselines only, per Sec 4.1 Baselines: 'we also include GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 as reference baselines to show the gap between small LLMs and advanced LLMs')
models.key_results: Table 2: GPT-3.5 VFF level-1/2/3 = 62.93 / 34.07 / 16.40; IFEval Prompt/Instruction = 56.56 / 67.51; InfoBench Avg. = 86.71 (marked as taken from Sun et al. 2024). GPT-4-turbo VFF level-1/2/3 = 76.29 / 53.33 / 35.31; IFEval Prompt/Instruction = 79.71 / 85.67; InfoBench Avg. = 89.42 (from Sun et al. 2024).

models.name: LLaMA-2-13B / LLaMA-2-70B / LLaMA-3-70B / Qwen-1.5-7B / WizardLM-7B (untrained baselines)
models.strategies: unverified — missing from extraction (no explicit prompting-strategy detail beyond 'baseline' zero/few-shot direct generation)

models.name: Mistral-7B, LLaMA-2-7B, LLaMA-3-8B (baseline vs. 'ours' progressively trained with self-generated SFT+DPO data)
models.strategies: Progressive self-improvement training: (1) Response Sampling with k=4 samples per instruction, using one self-generated wrong response as a one-shot demonstration to improve sampling efficiency (Sec 3.2, Response Sampling); (2) Training Data Annotation via the verifiable Python checker I to split responses into preferred y_w / dis-preferred y_l (Sec 3.2); (3) Progressive Training via SFT then DPO (beta=0.1), scaling level-1 -> level-2 -> level-3, each stage initialized from the prior stage's checkpoint (Sec 3.2, Progressive Training; Table 5 ablation). Training used LoRA (rank=64, alpha=128) on query/value attention heads, AdamW lr=5e-6, cosine schedule, batch size 4, 8 epochs, on NVIDIA A6000 GPUs, up to 1 hour (Sec 4.1, Training Settings).
models.key_results: Table 2: Mistral-7B baseline level-1/2/3 = 52.18/22.82/9.49 -> (ours) 61.85/32.66/15.97; IFEval P/I baseline 40.85/50.84 -> (ours) 37.50/51.19 (Prompt score decreased); InfoBench Avg baseline 72.84 -> (ours) 70.48 (decreased). LLaMA-2-7B baseline level-1/2/3 = 50.91/22.01/9.31 -> (ours) 57.59/27.72/13.28; IFEval P/I baseline 31.42/44.96 -> (ours) 40.48/54.08; InfoBench Avg baseline 12.98 -> (ours) 69.59 (large increase). LLaMA-3-8B baseline level-1/2/3 = 60.36/31.86/15.81 -> (ours) 85.56/59.67/38.36 (level-3 ours 38.36 exceeds GPT-4-turbo's 35.31, cited in Sec 4.2 'Effectiveness of Enhancing Format Control': 'the trained LLaMA-3-8B model outperforms GPT-4 in the level-3 format following task'); IFEval P/I baseline 68.22/77.14 -> (ours) 68.50/77.24; InfoBench Avg baseline 83.10 -> (ours) 78.13 (decreased).

headline_numbers: Table 2 (Sec 4.2): Prevalent Limitations — 7B-level open-source baselines score far lower on VFF level-2/level-3 (e.g., LLaMA-3-8B baseline: 31.86 / 15.81) and on IFEval than on general instruction-following (InfoBench), while GPT-4-turbo also drops sharply across levels (76.29 -> 53.33 -> 35.31), establishing a difficulty ladder effect common to all model sizes.

Table 2: best trained small model, LLaMA-3-8B (ours), reaches VFF level-1/2/3 = 85.56/59.67/38.36, surpassing GPT-4-turbo's level-3 score of 35.31 (Sec 4.2, 'Effectiveness of Enhancing Format Control').

Table 4 (Sec 5.2, Sampling Efficiency): cumulative one-shot-demonstration resampling reaches 78.80% correct-response accuracy (Total row, 7775/9865 correct) across 4 rounds, versus 55.14% (5440/9865) for 'Direct' (sampling multiple times with no demonstration) — per-round accuracy for the demonstration approach decays sharply after the first attempt (70.40% -> 23.05% -> 4.63% -> 2.47%).

Table 5 (Sec 5.2, Training Strategies): progressive SFT+DPO strictly dominates SFT-only or DPO-only at every level; DPO-only stays roughly flat/slightly declines across the L1->L2->L3 progressive stages (L1 DPO-Only: 63.26/37.14/20.29 -> L2 DPO-Only: 62.71/34.81/18.80 -> L3 DPO-Only: 62.25/34.73/17.95), i.e. it fails to compound gains across rounds the way SFT-DPO does (note: L3 DPO-Only 62.25/34.73/17.95 is still slightly ABOVE the untrained LLaMA-3-8B baseline's 59.75/32.19/16.66 on all three levels -- it does not fall below the original baseline, contrary to an earlier draft of this note; the paper's own claim is only that DPO-only 'will even harm the performance as the training proceeds' round-over-round, not that it drops below the pre-training baseline), while L3 SFT-DPO reaches 85.56/59.67/38.36 (matches Table 2's 'ours' row).

Table 6 (Sec 5.3, Accuracy & Time & Cost): on 200 samples, Python-based judgment = 100.0% accuracy, 0.52s, $0.0000 cost, versus GPT-4o-mini = 59.0% accuracy / 99.53s / $0.0144, and GPT-4o = 70.0% accuracy / 205.10s / $2.3830 — a ~100x speed advantage for Python judging, cited verbatim in text.

Table 7 (Sec 5.3, Consistency of LLM-based Judgment): GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini re-queried 50x on the same question/response show 25-52% inconsistency rates across temperatures 0.1-1.0 (GPT-4o: 25%/24%/33%/48% at temp 0.1/0.3/0.7/1.0; GPT-4o-mini: 33%/36%/44%/52%), demonstrating LLM judges are unreliable 'even by setting the temperature to 0.1.'

Sec 4.1 (Data): each VFF level curates 10k train / 7k test samples; the theoretical maximum producible instructions per level are ~60k (level-1), ~360k (level-2), ~2160k (level-3), given |Q|~52K Alpaca questions times summed candidate-value counts across ~60 meta constraints.
failure_modes: Sec 4.2 ('Prevalent Limitations'): all tested 7B-level open-source LLMs struggle specifically with level-2 and level-3 (multi-constraint, composed) format instructions on VFF and on IFEval, while performing acceptably on general instruction following (InfoBench) — i.e., failure is concentrated in constraint composition/stacking, not general instruction comprehension. Sec 4.2 ('Effectiveness of Enhancing Format Control'): after progressive training, Mistral-7B and LLaMA-3-8B show a slight decline on InfoBench (general instruction following) even though format control improves, hypothesized to be caused by (1) overfitting/regression from format-specific training relative to already-strong baselines, and (2) small test-set size (~500 samples) plus LLM-based-evaluation noise in InfoBench itself. Sec 5.2 (Table 4): response-sampling accuracy for correct/constraint-satisfying outputs collapses across repeated resampling attempts even with the one-shot wrong-demonstration trick (70.40% on attempt 1 down to 2.47% by attempt 4), showing diminishing returns / the model exhausting its 'easy' correct outputs quickly. Sec 5.2 (Table 5): DPO-only training (without prior SFT) actively harms format-following performance as training proceeds across levels. Sec 5.1 (Table 3, Quality of VFF dataset): randomly pairing questions and constraints introduces measurable Conflict scores (rising from 1.0-1.5 at level-1 to 2.0-2.5 at level-3 on a 5-point scale) and difficulty rises with constraint count (1.8 to 4.0), i.e., some fraction of generated instructions are internally contradictory or become harder simply by more constraints being stacked, not by the constraints' subtlety. Limitations section item 4: 'the results indicate that our training method may slightly harm some general instruction following performance, which needs more investigation.'
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's central inference is: in a single forward pass, small (7B-level) open-source LLMs can satisfy one format constraint tolerably but composition of multiple simultaneous constraints (their VFF level-2/level-3 setting) causes sharp accuracy collapse, and this collapse is present even for GPT-4-turbo, just less severe (Table 2, Sec 4.2). Multi-step improvement comes only on the training side, not via an evaluation-time interaction loop: the paper's 'progressive training' (Sec 3.2) trains the model once per difficulty level using self-generated, checker-annotated SFT+DPO data, then re-samples/fine-tunes at the next level from that checkpoint — this is a training curriculum across levels, not a multi-turn inference-time protocol; at test time each VFF/IFEval/InfoBench instance is still answered in one single-shot generation. The paper also finds (Table 4) that giving the sampler one self-generated wrong response as a one-shot demonstration during the training-data-collection stage substantially raises the yield of constraint-satisfying responses versus naive resampling, i.e., a one-shot demonstration helps generation quality, but this is used only to build the training set, not as part of the evaluated model's inference procedure.

relation_to_candidate.position: VFF is single-shot at test time: one instruction (question + concatenated meta-constraints) in, one free-text response out, scored 1 iff a conjunction of Python-verifiable checkers (I = product of F_k(y) over the c constraint instances) all pass — the same strict-AND, all-or-nothing scoring philosophy c22 specifies. The model observes only the final composed instruction (no partial feedback, no interaction turns) in a single forward pass; VFF's 'multi-step' element is entirely on the training-data side (progressive curriculum across difficulty levels via self-generated SFT+DPO data), not the evaluation protocol. Output shape is free-text natural-language generation (answers to Alpaca-style questions), not a single categorical label — closer to c22's 'few words' output than to a classification task. Verifier type is deterministic Python functions per meta constraint (F_k), composed by conjunction (I = prod F_k(y)), i.e., exactly c22's 'deterministic checker library' model, with per-constraint verdicts available as diagnostics (the paper computes I as a product of individually-computed F_k values, matching c22's 'per-checker verdicts logged as diagnostic facts').
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Very close structural overlap with c22's core mechanism: (1) constraints are drawn from a fixed pool of meta-constraint templates with variables and candidate values (Sec 3.1: C_k, V_k, F_k tuples) instantiated at generation time, matching c22's 'checker library' with parametrized atoms (word count, letter/keyword, casing, end-token style constraints — VFF's category taxonomy in Figure 2 includes 'limited word count,' 'limited punctuation,' 'limited grammar' (active/passive voice), 'limited structure' (JSON/YAML), 'specific number format,' and 'limited content'). (2) Multi-level stacking of up to 3 independently-checkable constraints per instance (VFF's level-1/2/3), directly matching c22's '3-5 composed constraints' and 'difficulty dial via constraint count.' (3) Strict conjunctive (AND, no partial credit) scoring: I = product of F_k(y), which is exactly c22's '1 iff ALL checkers pass (0/1, no partial credit).' (4) The paper's own Sec 5.3 empirically validates the premise underlying c22's design choice to use deterministic Python checkers rather than LLM judges: 100% accuracy, 100x faster, free, versus 59-70% accuracy for GPT-4o-mini/GPT-4o judges, with 25-52% self-inconsistency across repeated queries — direct evidence for why c22 should not use an LLM-as-judge oracle.
relation_to_candidate.differences: VFF has no conditional/Selection constraints (the 'if property P then rule A else rule B' branch c22 requires be discovered rather than copied) — VFF's meta constraints are static template-fill instantiations (e.g., 'Respond in [[VAR1]] language' with VAR1 drawn from a fixed value set), each independently statable in the prompt; nothing in the extracted text describes a constraint whose applicable rule is conditional on a property of the base question that the prompt does not disclose. This is the single largest structural difference and is exactly the mechanism c22's own docs identify as carrying 'the whole anti-enumeration load' (c22.md, Implementation lens). VFF's base task is any of ~52K real Alpaca instruction-following questions (open-ended, e.g., 'design a 3D house model'), not a deliberately trivial micro-task reduced to a few words of output — VFF answers can be long-form prose, unlike c22's 'output is a few words' framing. VFF's contribution is majority a training methodology (self-improvement SFT+DPO pipeline) for improving small-model format-following capability, whereas c22 is purely an evaluation/task-generation design; VFF's dataset itself, while synthetically instantiable, was built once and released as a fixed HuggingFace dataset (jinqij/VFF) with ~60 meta constraints authored via a GPT-4-assisted, then manually filtered process (Sec 3.1) — it is not described as an infinite on-the-fly generator with a seeded RNG the way c22's design requires; no COLLIE-style grammar or IFBench-style OOD-atom resistance to one-shot enumeration is present in VFF.
relation_to_candidate.candidate_addition: c22 would add: (1) the conditional Selection constraint mechanism (absent in VFF) that forces the model to discover a hidden branch rule rather than copy stated atoms, which is the anti-enumeration defense VFF entirely lacks; (2) a synthetic, seeded, infinite-pool generator (COLLIE-style grammar) rather than VFF's fixed, pre-released ~60-constraint dataset instantiated once over Alpaca questions, giving contamination resistance VFF does not claim; (3) deliberately trivial base tasks (a few words of output) rather than VFF's open-ended Alpaca questions, isolating the constraint-following signal from content-quality/relevance variance that VFF's own Sec 5.4 case study shows still matters (content quality and format quality were evaluated jointly in Table 8); (4) IFBench-style out-of-domain atoms explicitly selected to resist one-shot enumeration, beyond VFF's fixed in-domain-vs-IFEval out-of-domain split.

corpus_claim: VFF / Verifiable Format Control (Wang et al., Findings of NAACL 2025) provides 64 fully Python-checkable format meta-constraints and documents the low small-model floor cited by c22's adaptation notes.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported, with a numeric correction. The paper documents the low small-model floor accurately: Table 2 and Sec 4.2 ('Prevalent Limitations') directly support that 7B-level open-source LLMs have severely degraded format-following accuracy on level-2/level-3 (composed) constraints (e.g., LLaMA-3-8B baseline drops to 31.86/15.81 on level-2/3), so that half of the claim is supported. However, the constraint-count figure is miscited: the paper states 'we only reserve about 60 unique meta constraints' (Sec 3.1, 'Verifiable Meta Constraint'), not 64. No occurrence of the number 64 appears anywhere in the extracted verbatim text (abstract, all sections, tables, or captions). The corpus claim's '64 fully Python-checkable format meta-constraints' should read '~60 unique meta constraints' per the paper's own wording; this may be a rounding/transcription drift from a different corpus source and should be corrected before further reuse.
baseline_relevant: Instance/difficulty dial used: constraint count per instance c in {1,2,3} ('levels'), randomly selecting up to 3 unique meta constraints from the ~60-constraint pool and instantiating variables from each constraint's candidate-value set (Sec 3.1); difficulty (Table 3) and accuracy (Table 2) both move monotonically with level.

Dataset scale actually curated: 10k train + 7k test samples per level (Sec 4.1, Data) — theoretical max producible instances per level ~60k/360k/2160k respectively, given the |Q|=52K Alpaca question pool times summed |V_k| across constraints, but the paper deliberately subsamples rather than using the full producible space, citing training cost/diversity tradeoffs.

Metric/scoring formula: strict conjunctive pass — I = product over k=1..c of F_k(y), where each F_k is an independent Python bool function per constraint instance; I=1 only if the response satisfies every constraint simultaneously (Sec 3.1) — matches c22's 0/1 all-pass design exactly.

Prompt/format effects on accuracy: one-shot demonstration (a self-generated wrong response shown before resampling) raised correct-response yield substantially versus naive multi-sample 'Direct' resampling for training-data collection (Table 4: cumulative 78.80% vs. 55.14% correct, Sec 5.2) — relevant if our baseline considers giving models a negative exemplar to improve constraint-satisfaction rate, though this was used for training-data curation, not evaluation-time prompting.

Floors/ceilings observed: GPT-4-turbo (frontier ceiling reference) still only reaches 35.31% on VFF level-3 (Table 2); best trained 7B model (LLaMA-3-8B, ours) reaches 38.36% on level-3, i.e., even after targeted training, level-3 (3-constraint) accuracy sits well under 40% for both frontier and specialized-trained small models, evidence that stacking 3+ independent constraints under strict-AND scoring drives the ceiling down hard regardless of scale.

Verifier cost/reliability floor for LLM judges: Table 6 shows GPT-4o-mini and GPT-4o judges achieve only 59.0% and 70.0% accuracy respectively against ground truth (vs. Python's 100.0%), at ~100x-400x higher latency and nonzero dollar cost, plus 25-52% self-inconsistency across repeated queries (Table 7) — strong evidence for building/baselining exclusively with a deterministic Python checker oracle rather than any LLM-as-judge fallback.

Contamination/data-release status: VFF dataset is publicly released at https://huggingface.co/datasets/jinqij/VFF (Sec 3.1) as a fixed, pre-generated dataset (not an on-the-fly generator script in the paper's own release), so any reuse for a genuinely fresh/contamination-resistant baseline would need a re-implementation of the instantiation procedure (question + constraint sampling) rather than reusing the released files verbatim if contamination avoidance matters.

Data-quality caveat relevant to baseline design: Table 3 shows human/GPT-4-rated conflict scores between the randomly-paired base question and constraints rise from ~1.0-1.5 (level-1) to ~2.0-2.5 (level-3) on a 5-point scale, i.e., naive random pairing of base task + constraints introduces a nontrivial and growing rate of internally-contradictory instances as more constraints stack — the paper mitigated this partly via manual filtering (Sec 3.1: 'we also take manual check for each generated sample'), a design cost worth budgeting for if c22's generator does similar random composition.
quotes: "In this paper, we first curate a fully verifiable format following dataset VFF. In contrast to existing works often adopting external LLMs for instruction-following validations, every sample of VFF can be easily validated with a Python function." (Abstract)

"Finally, to maximize the applicability and universality of our meta constraints, we only reserve about 60 unique meta constraints, which are publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/jinqij/VFF." (Sec 3.1, Verifiable Meta Constraint)

"the corresponding binary functions F_{k\u2264c}(\u2217) can serve together to verify the correctness of the generated response y with I = \u220f_{k=1}^{c} F_k(y), where I = 1 represents as the model can fully adhere to this format control instruction x." (Sec 3.1, Format Following Dataset)

"The results first suggest that 7B level open-source LLMs struggle with level-2 and level-3 format following instructions of our VFF dataset and IFEval, while they commonly have acceptable performance on general instruction following dataset InfoBench." (Sec 4.2, Prevalent Limitations)
"Notably, the trained LLaMA-3-8B model outperforms GPT-4 in the level-3 format following task." (Sec 4.2, Effectiveness of Enhancing Format Control)

"The results show that in the format following evaluation scenario, Python based method has a significant advantage over LLM-based approach even with lightweight LLMs (GPT-4o-mini), especially considering the 100x speed up." (Sec 5.3, Accuracy & Time & Cost)

"The results suggest that LLMs are not reliable even by setting the temperature to 0.1. Moreover, the advanced GPT-4o is still inconsistent in judging the correctness of format following, which shows the limitations of LLM-based evaluations." (Sec 5.3, Consistency of LLM-based Judgment)

"For each level of VFF dataset, we curate 10k and 7k samples for training and testing, respectively... the ideal maximum numbers of producible instructions for each level of VFF are about 60k, 360k and 2160k." (Sec 4.1, Data)

corrections: novelty: corrected a false claim that only FollowBench/FCS/VFF carry the 'ML' (multi-level) mark in Table 1. Per the source table's actual checkmarks, IFEval also carries ML=yes, and IFEval's full mark row (Verifiable=yes, ML=yes, HJ=no, LJ=no, AJ=yes) is IDENTICAL to VFF's row -- so the dossier's claim that 'VFF is presented as the only benchmark with both properties simultaneously' is not supported by the paper's own Table 1. Reworded to state IFEval shares the same verifiable+multi-level combination, and to note VFF's actual distinguishing claims (broader constraint categories incl. JSON, larger combinatorial dataset scale) per the paper's own prose (Sec 2.1).

headline_numbers (Table 5 entry): corrected a false numeric comparison. The entry claimed L3 DPO-Only (62.25/34.73/17.95) was 'below the untrained LLaMA-3-8B baseline's 59.75/32.19/16.66 on levels 2-3' -- verified against Table 5 that L3 DPO-Only is actually HIGHER than the untrained baseline on all three levels (62.25>59.75, 34.73>32.19, 17.95>16.66). Reworded to describe the real pattern: DPO-Only accuracy stays roughly flat/slightly declines across the L1->L2->L3 progressive stages (63.26/37.14/20.29 -> 62.71/34.81/18.80 -> 62.25/34.73/17.95) rather than compounding across rounds the way SFT-DPO does, which matches the paper's actual claim ('it will even harm the performance as the training proceeds') without the false below-baseline comparison.
_verification.notes: Verified every number in Table 2, Table 3, Table 4, Table 5, Table 6, Table 7 against the source markdown's verbatim table transcriptions; all matched except the Table 5 comparison above. Verified all 8 verbatim quotes occur in the source text exactly as quoted. Verified protocol_mode classification (single-shot) -- confirmed no interactive/multi-turn evaluation track exists anywhere in the source text (checked for 'interactive', 'multi-turn', 'dialogue', 'feedback loop', 'iterative refine', etc. -- only hits were in bibliography entries for unrelated cited works, e.g. CRITIC and LLM-eval). Verified observation_content's claim that Figures 1/4-9 and Algorithm 1 are not extractable as text in this source file -- confirmed genuine via the source file's own 'Figures not reproduced' section. Verified the corpus_claim_verdict's existing correction (64 -> ~60 meta constraints) is itself correct per source Sec 3.1 wording ('we only reserve about 60 unique meta constraints'); the apparent 'D_M = {...| 1<=k<=16}' formal notation earlier in the same subsection is the SEED pool before GPT-4-assisted extension, not a contradiction, per the paper's own narrative order (seed pool -> extend via GPT-4 in-context learning -> ~60 final).
